sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presents with right elbow pain that has been present for the last three weeks.
He attributes the pain to increased tennis activity, having recently taken up playing tennis again.
The pain is described as sharp, stabbing, shooting, and aching, and is worse with movement and better with rest.
The patient has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they have not provided significant relief.
He also reports mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
The patient has a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two, mild intermittent asthma, and a surgical history of gallbladder removal 10 years ago.
He has an allergy to penicillin.
Review of systems: No fever is reported.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day, Flovent (fluticasone) 2 puffs twice a day, Albuterol as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The physical exam is normal with a few exceptions.
The patient has a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, which is unchanged from prior exams.

On musculoskeletal exam, the patient has moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, moderate pain with passive range of motion of the right elbow, and mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
No palpable joint effusion is noted.
Test Results: The right elbow x-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality, no malalignment or sign of joint effusion, and is otherwise normal.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, due to overuse likely from increased tennis activity.
Problem list: Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow; Diabetes type two; Mild intermittent asthma.
Plan:
I prescribed ultram 50mg once a day for pain management.
I recommended that the patient rest their right arm as much as possible, avoid repetitive stress in the right arm, and ice the right elbow for 20 minutes at least 3 times a day.
I also ordered a hemoglobin A1c level, a CBC blood test, and a chem-12 blood test.
I increased the patient's metformin to 1000mg twice a day and prescribed a refill of albuterol.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
